title: Fantasy Adventure Complications
description: The mid-operation wrench — things that go sideways in the middle of a plan, a mission, a dungeon, or a negotiation. Not failures, just complications: new information, changed conditions, third parties, and the world being more complex than anticipated.

entries.result: The person the party was sent to rescue has already been rescued by someone else and is now in a different kind of trouble.
A patron's instructions were accurate when given but have since been overtaken by events, and the patron hasn't been reached yet.
The party's contact in a city has moved on without warning. A note at their former address gives an alternate, which leads somewhere unexpected.
An NPC who was an obstacle is now an ally for reasons the party didn't anticipate and can't entirely trust.
The item the party recovered is not what the patron needed. The patron explains, upon inspection, what they actually need, which is different and adjacent.
A third party has taken an interest in what the party is doing — not hostile, not helpful, just observing — and the party doesn't know who they are or what their observation means.
The enemy the party has been preparing to fight has made a unilateral decision to ally with them against something they haven't been told about yet.
The party's route has been compromised. Their destination is the same. The way there is not.
Someone who was supposed to be on the party's side has been replaced without the party's knowledge by someone who looks and sounds similar.
The dungeon the party is clearing has been partially cleared by someone else who is currently still in it.
A piece of information the party received was accurate but was also specifically released to them by someone who knew they'd receive it.
The party's cover has been partially blown. Someone knows they're not what they said they were. That someone hasn't decided what to do with this yet.
A local law the party was unaware of applies to what they just did. The law is enforced by an official who is debating whether to enforce it.
The person who hired the party has an agenda they haven't disclosed. This becomes apparent when the party completes the first half of the job and learns what the second half is.
An NPC the party dismissed as irrelevant has turned out to be the most important person in the situation, for reasons that were invisible at first introduction.
A tool, vehicle, or resource the party was relying on has been damaged, stolen, or destroyed before they needed it.
A deadline has been moved earlier without notice. The party has less time than the plan assumed.
Two different groups have hired the party for the same job without knowing about each other, and their definitions of 'job done' are incompatible.
The magic that was supposed to work in this location doesn't. The specific reason is discoverable, but it requires time they may not have.
The party's objective has moved. Its new location is known but getting there requires passing through a situation that wasn't part of the plan.
An ally has been arrested on a charge that is partly true. Clearing them requires revealing something they were helping the party conceal.
A weather event — not supernatural, just large — has changed the tactical situation significantly.
Someone is following the party. They're not hostile. They're being followed themselves by someone who is.
The party has been operating on an assumption that has turned out to be wrong. Every plan that depends on that assumption needs revision.
A crowd is in the way at a moment when the party needs to move quickly and discretely through a specific space.
The thing the party thought was their biggest problem is actually their second biggest problem. The first has just become apparent.
An NPC who was supposed to provide support has their own crisis that needs immediate attention before they can help.
A map or plan the party was working from has an error. The error is in a critical location. The correct version exists but is not where the party is.
The party's communication with their patron has been intercepted. What was transmitted isn't sensitive, but the fact of the interception is.
An item the party was carrying has turned out to be something other than what it was described as. The difference is consequential.
A local group has legitimate authority over what the party is trying to do, and they've arrived to exercise it.
The person the party needs to speak to has gone into a meeting and cannot be interrupted. The meeting is with someone who has reason to know what the party is up to.
An earlier decision by the party has had an effect that is now visible in the current situation, positive or negative, that wasn't anticipated.
The party needs to be in two places at once. The timeline doesn't allow for sequential completion. This requires either splitting the party or choosing.
A rumor about the party has preceded them. The rumor is partly accurate, which is worse than wholly false.
The thing the party is looking for has been moved, sold, given away, or otherwise relocated since the most recent intelligence was gathered.
A secondary objective has become the primary one because the primary objective has become impossible through circumstances the party couldn't control.
An NPC the party has been treating as background has an opinion about what the party is doing and has decided to act on it.
The party has been given credit for something they didn't do, which has created expectations they may or may not want to meet.
A previously safe location has become unsafe. The party needs a new safe house, rest stop, or fallback position.
The person who was the problem has been replaced by someone who is worse. The replacement has happened while the party was dealing with the first person.
Evidence the party was relying on has been destroyed, and the party is now in a position where their assertion is their only support.
A figure from a party member's past appears at an inopportune moment with a claim on their attention that is either legitimate or extremely well-crafted.
The party's actions have had an unintended consequence that has created a problem they now have a responsibility for.
Someone the party needs information from has taken a vow of silence, which is either sincere or strategic and the difference matters.
A resource the party paid for is not available when they try to use it. The person who provided it has a reason they consider valid.
A hostile party has obtained the same information the party was just given, and the party knows this because they were there when it happened.
An NPC reveals, at a critical moment, that they are not who they said they were. Their actual identity is relevant to the situation.
The party has been operating under a legal authority that turns out to be invalid in this jurisdiction, which is only now being raised.
A trap the party set for someone else has caught someone they weren't targeting, and the situation requires resolution.
An agreement the party made earlier has a condition that is now due, which they may have forgotten.
The party's success has attracted the attention of someone who now wants to either take what they've accomplished or prevent them from doing more.
A witness to something the party did is present in a location where their testimony would be damaging, and they've recognized the party.
Something in the environment has changed — flood, fire, magic, earthquake — that has closed a planned route and opened others.
A negotiation has broken down at exactly the moment the party needed it to succeed, for reasons that are partially the party's fault.
The party's patron has new information that changes the objective. The new information is good news for the patron and creates additional work for the party.
A person the party trusted has made a decision that benefits them at the party's expense, and they know the party is going to find out.
The party has been blamed for something that happened while they weren't there. The person who blames them is important.
Something the party needs requires a specialist they don't have. The closest available specialist has a prior commitment that the party may or may not be able to override.
An NPC has made a decision that the party advised against, and the consequences of that decision are now arriving.
The party has more information than their contact. Their contact is making a decision based on less information. The decision will be made before the party can update them.
A piece of equipment has failed. The failure is at a critical time. A workaround exists but is imperfect.
The party's reputation in this location has preceded them in a way that is accurate but unhelpful to the current situation.
A secret the party was keeping has been discovered by someone who is now deciding what to do with it.
The party has stumbled into the middle of someone else's operation, which has nothing to do with theirs, except that they're now a variable in it.
An NPC reveals something they know about the situation that contradicts what the party's patron told them. Both sources appear credible.
The party's approach has been anticipated. Not fully, but enough that their target has made a specific preparation the party didn't account for.
A condition attached to an agreement is more onerous in practice than it seemed in theory, and the other party is expecting compliance.
An authority figure the party needs to get past is not hostile but is doing their job correctly, which is exactly as obstructive as hostility in this situation.
Someone has made an honest mistake that has created a serious problem. The someone is not the party.
The party has been given responsibility for something they didn't ask for, by someone who can't be argued with about it.
A person the party was working against has offered to become useful, and the offer is genuine, and the party has to decide whether their previous position or this new one is more correct.
A cultural or religious event has changed the normal patterns of a location, making the plan the party was executing obsolete for the next several hours.
An object the party is carrying is illegal here, and they are about to be searched by someone doing their job.
Someone the party needs has fled, and their destination is known but represents a specific additional challenge.
An ally of the party has made an action the party didn't authorize, and the action has had consequences the party now has to manage.
The party is operating in a location where every action is being observed and recorded for reasons that will become apparent later.
A previously unhelpful lead has suddenly become the most relevant thing in the investigation, because a new piece of information changes what it meant.
The party has been working toward a goal that turns out to be more difficult in the final stage than the first stages suggested.
A person who should be on one side of a conflict turns out to be on both, for reasons they consider defensible.
The party's timetable was based on incorrect information. They have less time or more time than they thought, and the adjustment creates its own problems.
An NPC has been taken hostage. The hostage-taker has conditions. The conditions are achievable. Meeting them creates a different problem.
The party's plan required a specific condition to be true at a specific time. The condition is true. The time is wrong.
A resource the party earned through a previous action is being contested by someone with a partial claim.
An exit route has been closed. The alternative is longer and requires the party to move through a situation they were trying to avoid.
The patron's objective and the correct objective have diverged. The party is between them.
The party's discretion has slipped — nothing critical, but enough that they're now doing the work under observation that was supposed to happen without it.
Someone involved in the situation has made a good-faith error that has worsened it significantly, and they don't know yet.
A protective enchantment the party was relying on has been negated. The way it was negated tells them something about who knows what they're doing.
The party has successfully completed their objective. The success has created a situation that immediately requires attention.
A secondary character in the situation has been keeping records. The records are comprehensive. Someone knows the records exist.
The party's arrival has accelerated a timeline they didn't know existed, and the acceleration is not favorable.
A piece of information turns out to have two interpretations, both valid, and the party has been acting on the wrong one.
The opposition knows less than the party, but they've guessed one specific thing correctly, and the specific thing they've guessed is the most important part.
A diplomatic immunity or special status the party was relying on is challenged by an official who has good reason for the challenge.
The party's patron is now part of the problem they were hired to solve, and the party has just learned this.
An NPC the party dismissed has been doing useful work in parallel, and the work has uncovered something the party needs to know immediately.
The situation was already resolved before the party arrived, but in the wrong direction, and the window to reverse it is very small.
Every faction in the situation thinks the party is working for a different faction. All the factions are wrong. The party is about to be in a room where all the factions are represented.

The complication is that there is no complication — everything is going exactly as planned, which has never happened before, and that is either enormously good news or the setup for something nobody has planned for.
